items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.
Students assume librairie = library. It actually means BOOKSHOP. The French word for library is bibliothèque. Both relate to books but serve different purposes.

items.q: What is the most common informal way to form a yes/no question in French?
items.correct: Rising intonation (statement said as a question) or using 'est-ce que'.
items.why: Students learn formal inversion first. In conversation, French uses intonation or est-ce que far more often. Inversion sounds formal/written - 'Tu parles français?' is the everyday form.

items.q: Reported speech shifts present to which tense?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Imparfait. “Passe compose” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which preposition introduces the agent in passive?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Par. “Pour” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Letters at end of present participle in French?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
